title: Zheng He
text:
Doctoral Candidate (Dr. med.)
🏛 Starkuvienė Lab, BioQuant, Heidelberg University · Heidelberg 🏥 Medizinische Fakultät Mannheim, Heidelberg University · Mannheim
Supervised by Prof. Dr. med. Michael Keese & Prof. Dr. Vytautė Starkuvienė-Erfle Doktorvater: PD Dr. med. Martin Sigl

✉ zheng.he@bioquant.uni-heidelberg.de ✉ hezheng.surgeon@gmail.com 🎓 Google Scholar ORCID GitHub
My research focuses on the phenotypic switch of vascular smooth muscle cells (VSMCs) — the process by which VSMCs switch between contractile and synthetic states in vascular disease. I combine high-throughput bioinformatics analysis with wet lab validation to identify small molecule modulators of this transition, with potential implications for atherosclerosis and in-stent restenosis.
I am also involved in research on ABCB5⁺ mesenchymal stem cells and their regenerative potential.

title: Zheng He
text:
Research Interests


Vascular Biology VSMC phenotypic plasticity, contractile-to-synthetic transition, in-stent restenosis, atherosclerosis

Bioinformatics Bulk RNA-seq, scRNA-seq, Mendelian randomization, gene signature discovery, transcription factor analysis

Translational Research Small molecule drug candidates (Resveratrol, Galangin), stem cell differentiation, ABCB5⁺ MSC biology

title: About
text:
I am a medical doctor and doctoral candidate (Dr. med.) affiliated with two institutions at Heidelberg University. My primary research is conducted at BioQuant, Heidelberg University under the supervision of Prof. Dr. Vytautė Starkuvienė-Erfle, while my doctoral degree is registered at the Medizinische Fakultät Mannheim under Prof. Dr. med. Michael Keese, who oversees my research project, funding, and thesis. The formal Doktorvater is PD Dr. med. Martin Sigl.
My work bridges clinical vascular medicine and computational biology, focusing on the molecular mechanisms underlying VSMC phenotypic plasticity.
Before my doctoral studies, I worked as a cardiovascular surgeon at the Third Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical University (2020–2021), where I gained clinical experience in vascular and endovascular procedures.

title: Research
text:
Vascular smooth muscle cells (VSMCs) are highly plastic cells that can transition between a quiescent contractile phenotype and a proliferative/migratory synthetic phenotype. This switch is central to the pathogenesis of atherosclerosis and in-stent restenosis (ISR).
My doctoral work employs a multi-stage bioinformatics pipeline integrated with experimental validation to:

Discover gene signatures of the VSMC phenotypic switch using bulk RNA-seq datasets (GSE193817, GSE69637, GSE253003)
Identify candidate modulators through transcription factor activity analysis and drug-gene interaction networks
Validate key candidates (Resveratrol, Galangin) via Western Blot, immunofluorescence, and qPCR in primary human aortic smooth muscle cells (HAoSMC)

Key tools: R (limma, edgeR, WGCNA, clusterProfiler), Python, Seurat, GSVA

title: Research
text:
ABCB5⁺ Mesenchymal Stem Cells
ABCB5⁺ MSCs represent a rare population of skin-derived stem cells with potent immunomodulatory and regenerative properties. In collaboration with the Starkuviene lab, I contributed to a comprehensive review of their differentiation potential and clinical translation perspectives.

title: Research
text:
Methods







Category
Techniques




Wet Lab
Western Blot, Immunofluorescence, RT-qPCR, Wound Healing Assay, Cell Viability (CTB)


Bioinformatics
Bulk RNA-seq analysis, scRNA-seq, Mendelian Randomization, WGCNA, GSVA, Gene Ontology/KEGG enrichment


Cell Models
Primary HAoSMC, HeLa cells, ABCB5⁺ MSCs
